返回本批次

“人工智能民意调查”是假民意调查

"AI polls" are fake polls

www.natesilver.net 作者 7777777phil 分数 24 评论 5 2026/04/12 00:42
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 艾萨克·阿西莫夫的短篇小说《特许经营权》封面图片。唐纳德·特朗普第二次赢得总统大选几周后，我从伦敦（当时居住的地方）乘火车前往牛津，参加一个关于 2024 年大选民意调查和预测的会议。大多数与会者都是民意调查专家或学者，但我也观看了 Aaru 和 Electric Twin 的演示，这两家公司的业务可互换，称为合成采样、硅采样或创建合成受众。没有初创公司的行话，这意味着他们使用大型语言模型（LLM）通过让人工智能代理扮演调查受访者的角色来模拟对民意调查的反应。我已经听说过 Aaru，这要归功于一些标题引人注目的文章，比如在选举日之前的几个月里“没有人，没问题：人工智能聊天机器人比人类更好地预测选举”。该公司背后的人提出了一些重大的、有些人甚至可能会说牵强的主张，例如：“两年内，我们将模拟整个地球——从乌克兰农作物的种植方式到伊拉克石油生产的影响，通过马六甲海峡的贸易，以及巴尔的摩市长的选举。”当 Semafor 向 Aaru 的联合创始人卡梅伦·芬克 (Cameron Fink) 和内德·科 (Ned Koh) 询问我的老板时，他们说“我们尊重所有在我们之前的人。” Nate（正如他经常做的那样）在 Twitter 上分享了他的想法：Nate Silver@NateSilver538LOL 我希望有一种方法可以做空这项业务，这可能是我听过的最糟糕的人工智能用例。Ben Smith @semaforbenSorry @NateSilver538 https://t.co/xNTCJwSMmh8:57 PM · 2024 年 9 月 20 日 · 539K 浏览量87 条回复 · 52 条转发 · 1.5K Likes 当我们在牛津演讲时，Fink 和 Koh 对于这种来来回回的态度相对温和。他们甚至主动提出寄给我一件 T 恤，上面印有他们显然制作的内特的名言。我从未接受过他们的邀请，现在我有些后悔。这些合成样品公司有一段时间从我的视线中消失了，但它们仍然存在。事实上，Aaru 最近获得了 10 亿美元的估值。他们正在做的事情是否接近人工智能开发最重要的前沿？可能性不大，尤其是当 Anthropic 刚刚开发出一种非常擅长利用软件漏洞的模型时，该模型仅向 40 家公司发布。尽管如此，芯片采样越来越多地进入公众民意调查。 Axios 在 3 月份报道称，根据 Aaru 的调查结果，“大多数人相信自己的医生和护士”——但没有提及这句话中的“人”实际上是法学硕士。大约在同一时间，公众情绪研究所通过 114 个人工智能代理“提升”了 373 名真实调查受访者的在线样本。1（剧透警告：即使是 Electric Twin 的联合创始人也不认为这是一种特别站得住脚的方法。）像 Qualtrics 和 Ipsos 这样的民意调查公司也在开发合成数据面板。那么，我们应该如何看待这些……“民意调查”呢？让我们先澄清一件事：无论它们是什么，它们都不是该术语通常定义的民意调查。一方面，使用法学硕士本质上编造虚假的调查受访者听起来像是有史以来最愚蠢的想法，这种想法只会不完美地复制真实的民意调查，同时引入各种偏见。另一方面，随着法学硕士以惊人的、甚至令人震惊的速度进步，也许我已经是 24 岁高龄的恐龙了，因为我仍然想依靠与真实的人交谈的民意调查。我不会说合成样本完全无用。事实上，正如我稍后会提到的，有证据表明某些技术可以快速且廉价地复制主要调查结果。但某些公司的营销可能略显乐观。芬克在 2024 年表示：“到下一次大选发生时，传统民意调查将不再存在。”我们距离中期选举只有 206 天了，基于我每天仍然需要收集大量民意调查的事实，我认为他应该在采访前通过人工智能代理样本进行预测。2要了解为什么合成样本无法取代民意调查，这里有一个关于它们如何工作的快速入门。这些模型最简单的版本包括获得法学硕士（例如 ChatGPT 或 Claude），为其提供人口统计资料（例如，居住在犹他州、年薪 7 万美元的受过大学教育的白人女性），然后要求其回答调查问题。您使用不同的人口统计资料重复该过程数千次，最终得到综合调查响应的样本。私营公司使用的实际模型比这更复杂，通常是因为它们为每个代理纳入了更多的人口统计特征，并为他们提供了额外的信息。例如，Aaru 向代理商提供他们可能会消费的新闻和信息，而 Electric Twin 则整合了客户关于他们试图复制的受众的专有数据。 Electric Twin 的联合创始人本·华纳 (Ben Warner) 向我解释道：“我们有一个大量数据……例如，5,000 人。 “我们能否准确预测他们将如何回答另一个问题？”尽管如此，在不考虑成本、速度或准确性的情况下，很明显为什么合成样本无法取代民意调查。民意调查从根本上来说是一个数据收集过程。我们可以通过将调查纳入选举预测来进行预测，但民意调查的主要目的不是预测，而是收集有关人们想法和感受的新数据。另一方面，硅采样不会产生新数据。它只是一个模型：你输入 LLM 训练数据、人口统计提示和一堆其他信息，它会预测民意调查的内容。我们喜欢这里的模型，但这种差异对于我交谈过的大多数民意调查专家来说是一个重要的哲学症结：“我认为政治应该远离[合成抽样]，因为我们试图……代表人民的声音，”GQR Insights 的副总裁 Natalie Jackson 说。民意调查专家约翰·哈格纳告诉我：“我想我对这个想法非常怀疑。 I don’t think it’s research.那时，你要求机器告诉你你已经相信的事情。”哈格纳看过一些早期合成抽样实验的演示，但到目前为止，“如果它被用于竞选活动，人们会保持难以置信的安静。”3但是伊莱，我听到你说，民意调查本身不是越来越受到建模决策的控制吗？事实上，它们是：民意调查者对使用哪种抽样方法、如何定义可能的选民模型以及如何对样本进行加权的选择可以而且确实会导致他们发布的结果存在巨大差异。阿鲁甚至在孕产妇死亡率“民意调查”所包含的方法论声明中提到了这些局限性——尽管我在这里宽松地使用了“方法论声明”这个术语，因为它根本没有真正解释模型是如何工作的。我们可以忽略合成抽样不会受到一组单独的偏差的影响（坦率地说是荒谬的）。重要的一点是，对实际民意调查数据使用加权和其他统计技术与使用模型来预测民意调查内容之间仍然存在有意义的差异。后者更接近选举预测或 MRP 等技术——可能有用的模型，但不能替代民意调查。4公平地说，其他综合抽样公司对民意调查和模型之间的区别非常满意。华纳将民意调查和综合抽样与工具箱中的不同工具进行了比较。 “我认为我们犯的错误是，我们认为这些新工具应该以完全相同的方式工作，或者以某种方式取代这些旧工具，”他说。 “而不是认为，好吧，所以我们一直有锤子，我们一直有螺丝刀，现在我们有一把锯。但不要用锯子来尝试做锤子的工作。”NateEli 的简短评论并没有要求我发表评论——他很粗鲁，你不觉得吗？但自从我编辑这个故事以来，我想我应该添加一些快速的想法，而不是把话放在他嘴里。除了经常误导性的营销之外，人工智能“民意调查”炒作最让我困扰的是，人工智能工具使统计推断更便宜和/或更好（注你也许能够训练一个模型，对一些难以接触到的民意调查受访者的言论做出合理的估计——比如，一个投票给特朗普的年轻黑人（这样的人会在调查中勾选一些通常很难接触到的选民）。事实上，这与 Silver Bulletin 预测等模型已经做的事情密切相关，它们基本上通过使嘈杂的调查数据变得平滑。基于过去的投票模式或全国民意调查或其他州的调查得出的推论。但除非你直接接触到这些选民，否则你不会发现他们的想法。因此，如果我进行竞选活动，我会花更多的精力来寻找这些选民的代表性样本，然后我会聘请一些聪明的分析师来弄清楚。基于我的竞争对手无法获得的专有数据对竞选策略的影响——内特·西尔弗（Nate Silver）如果综合调查只是一种新型模型，那么下一个明显的问题是这些模型是否至少准确。这个问题的答案在很大程度上取决于你问的是谁。从一方面来看，你有一个极端的论点，即综合抽样比实际民意调查更好、更准确。 Koh 最近对 CNBC 表示：“即使你直接与客户交谈，人类行为也比你更重要。”在他看来，合成采样这不是民意调查的锤子，它是“魔法”。当然有证据表明合成样本可以复制某些调查结果。但如果阿鲁确实有任何他们的方法优于民调的例子，他们也不会透露。5 例如，阿鲁的 2024 年选举模型显示卡马拉·哈里斯于 11 月 4 日在密歇根州、内华达州、宾夕法尼亚州和威斯康星州领先。尽管他们已经删除了预测页面，但他们认为哈里斯在 11 月 2 日获胜的几率为 50.5%。 6 选举结束后，芬克告诉 Semafor，他对这些结果感到非常满意，因为它们“在误差范围内”，而这个术语在应用于人工智能代理的“样本”时毫无意义。当然，Aaru 表示他们的模型自 2024 年以来已经有所改进，所以现在它们应该比民意调查更准确吧？尽管如此，他们更有力的论点是成本：“我们比传统民意调查更快、更便宜，而且更准确，”芬克说。前两种说法无可否认是正确的，但第三种说法却把我们带到了相反的一端。杰克逊和哈格纳都怀疑这些模型是否可靠，远远超出了复制常见调查结果的范围。 “我只是……当我们寻求细致入微的观点时，我认为这些机器不是我们想要的。我的例子是 2024 年亚利桑那州和内华达州的人们投票支持特朗普，并在投票倡议中投票支持扩大堕胎范围，”杰克逊说。哈格纳发现了一个类似的问题：“我参加的会议上得到的报告是，对此的早期实验，他们无法让受访者像人类受访者一样具有种族主义或性别歧视，或者坦率地说，像人类受访者一样消极。”学术研究大多同意这一点。虽然有一些论文在使用法学硕士复制民意调查数据时显示出有希望的结果，但大多数论文表明法学硕士存在各种怪癖，例如产生太少的“不知道”回答，并且可能严重高估唐纳德·特朗普和卡马拉·哈里斯等政客的好感度。他们似乎还面临着人口亚群之间差异太小的问题，例如，民主党和共和党之间的预测意见差异太小。当我向华纳询问这些研究时，他对这些论文的回应是，仅仅因为学术界无法让合成抽样发挥作用，并不意味着该技术总体上不起作用。 “实际上，争论是，好吧，你的不起作用。这并不意味着……对于这套复杂的机器，它需要大量投资、大量时间、大量资金，你无法让它发挥作用。”摆在桌面上的牌，我有点同情这个论点，因为学者们并不擅长做出选举预测。通常，参与游戏的人是最准确的。 Warner 的观点是，Electric Twin 所采用的方法（例如，使用不同的模型和提示对每个合成受访者进行多次预测，然后对这些预测进行平均以获得最终预测，就像一种整体预测）比更简单的学术模型产生更好的结果。Warner 与我分享了他的方法与最近一篇学术论文中的方法之间的比较，Electric Twin 确实能够获得更准确的复制。但即便如此，他也承认合成采样“不是水晶球”。 “如果你问我，我认为使用其他数据源是否会比询问某人会投票给谁更准确，我可能会说不。但如果你问我‘你的系统对我们今天的投票率建模有用吗？’我会说是。”但无论好坏，这种方法似乎已经在市场研究领域变得越来越流行。阿鲁最近谈论的大多数客户都是安永和麦当劳等企业。这并不是说人工智能不会出现在政治投票过程的其他部分。民意调查机构已经在使用它来编码开放式调查回复，YouGov 等一些公司正在测试使用法学硕士向调查受访者询问问题。更令人担忧的是，实际民意调查面临的一个危险是人工智能代理可用于渗透在线调查。大多数在线民意调查都会使用各种检查来防止这种情况发生，但关于这些过滤器的有效性以及人工智能代理目前在在线调查中的流行程度，存在相互矛盾的证据。如果这些代理变得无法检测，则可能意味着在线民意调查的终结，但解决方案并不是用 ChatGPT 取代所有受访者。2您可能会说 Fink 意味着下一次总统选举，但 (a) 我也相信我们在 2028 年仍将进行真正的民意调查，(b) 在这种情况下，他应该要求法学硕士来定义“大选”。4事实上，Silver Bulletin 在我们的预测中不包括 MRP 产生的“民意调查”或平均值，我们认为当他们的从业者以暗示已收集原始数据的方式描述它们时，这是极具误导性的5 Aaru 和 EY 最近的一份报告确实展示了两个比调查更接近现实世界基准的综合估计的例子 - 但我对这些发现持保留态度，因为该报告读起来更像是一则广告，并且不涉及提前做出任何类型的预测。

Original Summary

  1. Cover image from Isaac Asimov’s short story “Franchise”.A few weeks after Donald Trump’s second presidential win, I took the train up from London (where I was living at the time) to Oxford to attend a conference on polls and forecasts of the 2024 election. Most of the attendees were pollsters or academics, but I also watched presentations from Aaru and Electric Twin, two companies that do what is interchangeably called synthetic sampling, silicon sampling, or creating synthetic audiences. Sans startup jargon, that means they use large language models (LLMs) to simulate responses to public opinion polls by having AI agents take on the role of survey respondents.I had already heard of Aaru thanks to some articles with eye-catching headlines like “No people, no problem: AI chatbots predict elections better than humans” in the months leading up to Election Day. The guys behind the company were making some big, some might even say far-fetched claims, such as: “within two years, we will simulate the entire globe — from the way crops are grown in Ukraine to how that impacts production of oil in Iraq, trade through the strait of Malacca, and elections for the mayor of Baltimore.” When Semafor asked Aaru’s cofounders — Cameron Fink and Ned Koh — about my boss, they said “we respect all those who came before us.” Nate (as he so often does) shared his thoughts on Twitter:Nate Silver@NateSilver538LOL I wish there were a way to short this business this is maybe the single worst use case for AI I've ever heard.Ben Smith @semaforbenSorry @NateSilver538 https://t.co/xNTCJwSMmh8:57 PM · Sep 20, 2024 · 539K Views87 Replies · 52 Reposts · 1.5K LikesFink and Koh were relatively good-natured about this back-and-forth when we spoke at Oxford. They even offered to mail me one of the t-shirts featuring Nate’s quote they apparently had made. I never took them up on the offer, which I now somewhat regret.These synthetic sampling companies fell off my radar for a while, but they do still exist. In fact, Aaru recently received a $1 billion valuation. Is what they’re doing anywhere close to the most important frontier in AI development? Not by a longshot, especially when Anthropic just developed a model so adept at exploiting software vulnerabilities that it’s only being released to 40 companies.Still, silicon sampling is increasingly finding its way into public polling. Axios reported in March that “a majority of people trust their own doctors and nurses” based on findings from Aaru — without mentioning that the “people” in that sentence were actually LLMs. Around the same time, the Public Sentiment Institute “boosted” their online sample of 373 real survey respondents with 114 AI agents.1 (Spoiler alert: even the co-founder of Electric Twin doesn’t think that’s a particularly defensible approach.) Polling companies like Qualtrics and Ipsos are also developing synthetic data panels.So, what should we make of these … “polls”? Let’s get one thing out of the way: whatever they are, they’re not polls in the way that term is usually defined.On one hand, using LLMs to essentially make up fake survey respondents sort of sounds like the dumbest idea ever, one that will only imperfectly replicate real polls while introducing all sorts of biases. On the other hand, with LLMs improving at a remarkable, perhaps even alarming rate, maybe I’m a dinosaur at the ripe old age of 24 because I still want to rely on polls that talk to actual people.I’m not going to argue that synthetic samples are completely useless. In fact, as I’ll return to later, there is evidence that some techniques can replicate topline survey results quickly and cheaply. But the marketing from certain companies can be slightly optimistic. “No traditional poll will exist by the time the next general election occurs,” said Fink in 2024. We’re just 206 days away from the midterms, and based on the fact that I still have to collect a bunch of polls every day, I’d say he should have run that prediction by a sample of AI agents before the interview.2To see why synthetic samples can’t replace polls, here’s a quick primer on how they work. The simplest version of these models involves taking a LLM (like ChatGPT or Claude), giving it a demographic profile (e.g., a white, college-educated woman who lives in Utah and makes $70k a year), and then asking it to respond to a survey question. You repeat that process a few thousand times using different demographic profiles and end up with a sample of synthetic survey responses.The actual models used by private companies are more sophisticated than this, usually because they incorporate more demographic characteristics for each agent and provide them with extra information. Aaru, for example, feeds agents a diet of news and information they’d be likely to consume, while Electric Twin incorporates their customers’ proprietary data about the audience they’re trying to replicate. The way Ben Warner, the co-founder of Electric Twin, explained it to me was “we have a large amount of data on… for instance, 5,000 people. Can we make an accurate prediction of how they would respond to another question?”Still, without any reference to cost, speed, or accuracy, it should be obvious why synthetic samples can’t replace polls. Polling is fundamentally a data collection process. We might use surveys to make predictions by feeding them into election forecasts, but the main purpose of a poll isn’t prediction, it’s gathering new data about what people think and how they feel. Silicon sampling, on the other hand, produces no new data. It’s simply a model: you input LLM training data, demographic prompts, and a bunch of other information, and it spits out a prediction for what a poll would say.We love models here, but models aren’t polls. That difference is an important philosophical sticking point for most pollsters I talk to. “I think politics should stay away from [synthetic sampling], because we’re trying to… represent the voice of the people,” said Natalie Jackson, a vice president at GQR Insights. Democratic pollster John Hagner told me “I think I’m just incredibly skeptical of this idea. I don’t think it’s research. At that point, you’re asking the machine to tell you what you already believe.” Hagner has seen some presentations of early synthetic sampling experiments, but so far, “if it’s being used in a campaign, people are keeping it incredibly quiet.”3But Eli, I hear you saying, aren’t polls themselves increasingly governed by modeling decisions? Indeed they are: pollsters’ choices on which sampling method to use, how to define their likely voter models, and how to weight their samples can and do lead to dramatic differences in the results they publish. Aaru even referenced these limitations in the methodology statement included with that maternal mortality “poll” — although I’m using the term “methodology statement” loosely here, because it doesn’t really explain how the model works at all.We can ignore the (frankly preposterous) implication that synthetic sampling isn’t subject to a separate set of biases. The important point is that there’s still a meaningful difference between using weighting and other statistical techniques on actual polling data and using a model to predict what a poll would say. The latter is far closer to election forecasts or techniques like MRP — potentially useful models, but not a replacement for polls.4To be fair, other synthetic sampling companies are perfectly happy with the distinction between polls and models. Warner compared polling and synthetic sampling to different tools in a toolbox. “The mistake I think we make is we think that these new tools should either work in exactly the same way or somehow replace these old tools,” he said. “Rather than thinking of it as, okay, so we’ve always had the hammer, we’ve always had the screwdriver, now we’ve got a saw. But don’t use a saw to try [to] do the job of a hammer.”A quick comment from NateEli didn’t ask me for a comment — rather rude of him, don’t you think? But since I’m editing this story, I figured I’d add a few quick thoughts rather than putting words in his mouth. Beyond the frequently misleading marketing, what bothers me the most about the AI “poll” hype is that as AI tools make statistical inference cheaper and/or better (note that these are not synonyms) that actually increases the comparative value of collecting original data. You might be able to train a model to make a reasonable estimate of what some hard-to-reach poll respondent would say — say, a young Black man who voted for Trump. (Such a person checks a number of boxes for a voter who is usually hard to reach in surveys.) Indeed, this is closely related to what models like the Silver Bulletin forecast already do. They essentially smooth out the kinks in noisy survey data by making inferences based on past voting patterns or national polls or surveys of other states.But you don’t actually know what these voters think unless you’re reaching them directly. If there’s a shift in opinion among this subgroup, you’re not going to detect it. So if I were running a campaign, I’d invest more in going the extra mile to find a representative sample of these voters. And then I’d hire some smart quants — perhaps with help from Claude et. al. — to figure out the implications for campaign strategy based on that proprietary data that my competitors didn’t have access to. -Nate SilverIf synthetic surveys are just a new type of model, the next obvious question is whether the models are at least accurate. The answer to that question depends very much on who you ask.On one end of the spectrum, you have the maximalist argument that synthetic sampling is better and more accurate than actual polls. “It’s an incredibly challenging problem to go to someone and say ‘hey, we’re going to be more accurate at predicting human behavior than you, even when you talk to your customers directly,” Koh recently told CNBC. In his view, synthetic sampling isn’t a saw to polling’s hammer, it’s “magic.”There’s certainly evidence that synthetic samples can replicate certain survey toplines. But if Aaru does have any examples of their approach outperforming the polls, they’re keeping those to themselves.5 Aaru’s 2024 election model, for example, had Kamala Harris leading in Michigan, Nevada, Pennsylvania, and Wisconsin on November 4th. And although they’ve since taken down their forecast page, they gave Harris a 50.5 percent chance of winning the race on November 2nd.6After the election, Fink told Semafor he was happy enough with those results because they were “within margin of error,” a term that is meaningless when applied to a “sample” of AI agents. And of course, Aaru says their models have improved since 2024, so supposedly now they’d be more accurate than the polls? Still, their stronger argument is on cost: “We are significantly faster and cheaper than traditional polling, and still more accurate,” said Fink. The first two claims there are undeniably true, but the third brings us to the opposite end of the spectrum.Both Jackson and Hagner are skeptical that these models are reliable for anything far beyond replicating common survey toplines. “I just… don’t think the machines are what we want when we’re looking for nuanced views. My example on this is people in Arizona and Nevada in 2024 who voted for Trump and voted for expanding abortion in their states on ballot initiatives,” said Jackson. Hagner identified a similar issue: “the reports that have come through at the meetings that I’ve been at are that the early experiments on this, they cannot get respondents to be as racist or sexist or, frankly, as negative as human respondents.”Academic research mostly agrees on this point. While there are some papers that show promising results when using LLMs to replicate polling data, most show that LLMs suffer from various quirks like producing too few “don’t know” responses and can seriously overpredict the favorability of politicians like Donald Trump and Kamala Harris. They also seem to struggle with too little variation between demographic subgroups, so the difference in predicted opinion between Democrats and Republicans, for example, is too small.When I asked Warner about these studies, his response to these papers was that just because academics can’t get synthetic sampling to work doesn’t mean that the technique doesn’t work in general. “Actually, the argument is, okay, yours does not [work]. That does not mean […] for this complex set of machinery, which uses a lot of investment, a lot of time, a lot of money, you can’t get it to work.”Cards on the table, I’m somewhat sympathetic to this argument because academics aren’t exactly great at making election forecasts. Usually, the people with skin in the game are the most accurate. Warner’s argument is that the approach Electric Twin takes — which includes, for example, making multiple predictions for each synthetic respondent using different models and prompts and subsequently averaging those to get a final prediction like a sort of ensemble forecast — produces better results than the simpler academic models.Warner shared a comparison between his method and the method from a recent academic paper with me, and Electric Twin was indeed able to get more accurate replication. But even still, he acknowledged that synthetic sampling “is not a crystal ball.” “If you asked me, do I think using other data sources will be more accurate than asking somebody who they will vote for, I would probably say no. But if you asked me ‘would your system be useful for our turnout modeling today?’ I would say yes.”But for better or worse, it looks like the method is already getting more popular in the market research world. Most of the clients Aaru talks about these days are businesses like EY and McDonald’s. That’s not to say AI won’t pop up in other parts of the political polling process. Pollsters are already using it to code open-ended survey responses, and some firms, like YouGov, are testing using LLMs to ask survey respondents questions.More worryingly, one danger to actual polls is that AI agents can be used to infiltrate online surveys. Most online polls use various checks to prevent that from happening, but there’s conflicting evidence on how effective those filters are and how prevalent AI agents currently are in online panels. If those agents ever become impossible to detect, it might spell the end of online polling, but the solution isn’t to replace all of your respondents with ChatGPT.2You could argue that Fink meant the next presidential election, but (a) I’m also confident we’ll still have real polls in 2028 and (b) in that case he should have asked an LLM to define “general election.”4Indeed, Silver Bulletin does not include “polls” produced by MRP in our forecasts or averages, and we think it’s extremely misleading when their practitioners describe them in a way that suggests original data had been collected among a large number of states or Congressional districts.5A recent report from Aaru and EY did show two examples of a synthetic estimate being closer than a survey to a real-world benchmark — but I’d take those findings with a grain of salt because the report reads more like an ad and didn’t involve any sort of prediction being made ahead of time.

评论（默认中文）

jncfhnb 2026/04/12 01:57
这整个合成采样的事情真的让我难以置信。就像……很难想出一个不像双曲稻草人的例子。就是这么愚蠢。
This whole synthetic sampling thing really boggles my mind. It’s like… it’s difficult to come up with an example that doesn’t feel like a hyperbolic strawman. It’s just that dumb.
在 HN 中查看
0xy 2026/04/12 02:15
人工智能民意调查是假的，真实的民意调查也是假的，内特·西尔弗的模型真的是假的。

内特·西尔弗 (Nate Silver) 暗示佛罗里达州在 2024 年对民主党有利，然后它的支持率上升了 +13 R。这是在他花了 9 年坚持民意调查之后，由于抽样偏差或害羞的选民，或者在其他情况下根本就是彻头彻尾的欺诈（塞尔泽的爱荷华州民意调查），该民调系统地低估了共和党的支持率。

AI polls are fake, real polls are fake, and Nate Silver's modeling is REALLY fake.

Nate Silver implied Florida was in play in 2024 for Democrats, then it went +13 R. This is after he spent 9 years clinging to polling which systematically undercounted Republican support due to either sampling bias or shy voters, or were simply outright fraud in other cases (Selzer's Iowa poll).

在 HN 中查看
jncfhnb 2026/04/12 02:32
这从根本上来说不是一个有意义的论点

您需要评估校准。不是“准确度”。 “在玩”到底是什么意思？

That’s just fundamentally not a meaningful argument

You need to assess calibration. Not “accuracy”. What does “in play” even mean?

在 HN 中查看
arc_light 2026/04/12 02:20
问题在于人工智能无法理解人类的情感或人群对突然的政治转变的反应方式。在一场活动中，人们会根据当时的情况改变想法，无论你提供多少数据，当事情真正开始升温时，机器总是会落后一步。
The issue is that AI doesn't understand human emotion or the way a crowd reacts to a sudden political shift. In the middle of a campaign, people change their minds based on the moment, and no matter how much data you feed it, a machine is always going to be a step behind when things actually start to heat up.
在 HN 中查看
jncfhnb 2026/04/12 02:34
不，不是真的。只是如果你问模型有多少人喜欢苹果，它不知道答案。我们甚至不需要达到意见改变的程度。

模型不会“轮询”他们的训练数据。如果你给它五个是的答案和五个否的答案，它不会认为它是 50/50。

如果你给它实际的数据，它可以导出它，但这将是一个民意调查

No not really. It’s just if you ask the model what percent of people like apples, it doesn’t know the answer. We don’t even need to get to the level of change of opinions.

Models don’t “poll” their training data. If you give it five yes answers and five no answers, it doesn’t think that it’s 50/50.

If you give it actual data it can derive it, but that would be a poll
